claim: Elderly, disabled, and small dogs tend to be recovered quickly, typically within a 1–2 mile radius of their escape point.
stance_target: distance-traveled norms by dog category — small/elderly dogs
scope_conditions: elderly, disabled, small dogs; radius varies with population density
quoted_support: In general, elderly dogs, disabled dogs, and small dogs tend to be recovered quickly, often ending up within a few blocks from their escape point. Your target search area will be within a 1 to 2-mile radius of your home.

claim: Aggressive, panicked, and skittish/shy dogs can travel for miles before intervention and require a 5–10 mile poster radius versus 1–2 miles for other dogs.
stance_target: distance-traveled norms by dog category — fearful/aggressive dogs
scope_conditions: dogs with aggressive, panicked, or skittish/shy temperaments
quoted_support: Aggressive dogs, panicked dogs (fireworks, involved in car accident), and dogs with skittish, shy temperaments will be more difficult to capture and are at risk of traveling further. These dogs will often run blindly and can travel for miles before intervention. The radius for your posters should, however, encompass a large area – a 5 to 10-mile radius in some cases.

claim: When approaching a panicked dog, walking straight toward it mimics predator stalking behavior — the correct approach is to shuffle sideways with hip facing the dog, stop at the alert zone, and speak in varied tones without advancing.
stance_target: approach body language protocol to avoid triggering flight response
scope_conditions: panicked or skittish lost dogs during approach attempts
quoted_support: We often think that if we creep slowly, a dog will understand that we are not going to hurt it when in reality we are simulating how a predator would slowly stalk its prey. Thus when you reach the 'Alert Zone', you should stop, talk normally in various tones to the dog, and do not advance forward. As the dog becomes accustomed to your presence, then you can advance a foot or two, never walking straight at the dog, always shuffling sideways with your hip facing the dog.

claim: There are three zones when approaching a displaced panicked dog: (1) Awareness Zone — dog is aware of your presence; (2) Alert Zone — body language changes, dog looks for escape; (3) Action Zone — dog will fight or flee; it is at the action zone that snappy snares/catchpoles can be used.
stance_target: spatial framework for controlled approach to panicked lost dog
scope_conditions: trained MAR technicians or experienced rescuers approaching panicked dogs
quoted_support: There are three zones when approaching a displaced, panicked dog: (1) The Awareness Zone (2) The Alert Zone (3) The Action Zone. The Awareness Zone is where the animal is first aware that you are there. When moving closer towards the dog, the Alert Zone is when the dog's body language begins to change. At this point the dog might start looking for a way to escape.

claim: Panicked dogs that won't respond to their owner may still approach or accept approach from a 'magnet dog' (a familiar or extremely friendly dog on a 30-foot leash) as an attraction and distraction device.
stance_target: magnet dog technique as alternative capture method for owner-avoidant panicked dogs
scope_conditions: panicked dogs who bolt from owner; magnet dog must be familiar to target dog or highly friendly
quoted_support: Even though they don't recognize or respond to their owner, sometimes they are willing to approach or to be approached by another dog. You can use a second dog (on a 30-foot long leash) as an attraction device to distract your dog so you can get close enough to capture him.

claim: Cell phone to cell phone coordination between a witness spotting a loose dog and the owner in real time has proven to be a highly effective lost dog recovery method, given how quickly loose dogs can move.
stance_target: real-time sighting coordination as high-value recovery tactic
scope_conditions: witnesses with cell phones who maintain visual on the dog while alerting the owner
quoted_support: Ask the caller if they are calling from a cell phone and if they are, ask them to remain on the phone with you to keep you updated on the dogs' location. This tactic alone (cell phone to cell phone communication between a witness and a dog owner) has proven to be a highly effective method of recovering lost dogs.
